content.text: accepts two forms: a direct value (
— a function that receives the latest pending state and returns the next state (

). The functional form is essential whenever the new value depends on the previous one, especially when multiple updates happen in quick succession or inside async callbacks where the state snapshot may be stale.
The Problem: Stale Snapshots with Direct Values

Every render captures state as a constant — a snapshot. Calling the setter with a direct value computes the new state from that snapshot. If you call the setter multiple times in one event handler, each call reads the same snapshot, so the updates do not stack.
React batches the three calls and processes the queue after the handler exits. Because all three calls use
in every snapshot, each queued instruction says "replace the value with 1". The last one wins and
When you pass a function to the setter, React does not call it immediately. Instead, it adds it to the update queue. When React processes the queue, it calls each updater in order, passing the
as the argument — not the original snapshot.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing direct value vs functional update: direct value uses stale snapshot (1+1=1), functional update chains correctly (0→1→2)

content.text: Each updater function receives the pending value from the previous step in the queue — not the snapshot from the render. React processes them as a chain, so the increments compose correctly.
React starts with the current committed state value (the snapshot from the last completed render).
It takes the first item in the queue. If it is a direct value, the pending value becomes that value. If it is an updater function, React calls it with the pending value and uses the return value.
It repeats step 2 for every item in the queue, each time using the result of the previous step as
The final pending value becomes the new state for the next render.
Direct values and updater functions can be mixed freely in the same queue. A direct value resets the pending value; subsequent updater functions apply on top of it.
When to Use the Functional Form
Avoids relying on potentially stale snapshot
Setter called multiple times in one handler
Setter called inside async callback (setTimeout, Promise)
Snapshot from render time is stale by then
Setting to a known fixed value
Direct value is simpler and equally correct
The initial value doesn't depend on previous state
Functional Updates with Arrays and Objects
The pattern extends naturally to arrays and objects. The updater function receives the current array or object and must return a
value — never mutate the argument directly.
Because each handler uses the functional form, calling
in rapid succession — or from an async callback — will always produce the correct final array.

capture the state snapshot from the render in which they were created. If state has changed by the time the callback runs, the snapshot is stale. The functional form bypasses this entirely because the updater always receives the
at the time React processes the queue.
With the direct form and an empty dependency array,
inside the interval — and the counter would forever display
. The functional form avoids this without needing to re-register the interval on every render.
Async Data Fetch with Functional Update
Purity Requirement: Updater Functions Must Be Pure
React may call your updater function twice in Strict Mode (development only) to detect side effects. The function must be pure: given the same
value it must always return the same next value, with no side effects.
must be a pure function: no mutations, no network calls, no logging that has observable side effects. Compute and return only.
The updater parameter name is arbitrary. Use names that make the function self-documenting.

setCount(prev => prev + 1);      // common for primitives
setCount(c => c + 1);            // abbreviated
setTodos(todos => [...todos, x]); // mirrors the state name
setUser(user => ({ ...user, name })); // mirrors the state name
or matching the state variable name (e.g.
) are both widely used and clearly communicate intent.
Worked Example: Optimistic UI with Rollback

Optimistic updates show the expected result immediately, then roll back on error. Functional updates make the rollback precise — remove only the item that was optimistically added, even if other items were added concurrently.

If the user clicks the button multiple times before any request resolves, the functional form ensures each click and rollback operates on the correct cumulative value — something impossible to achieve cleanly with direct values.
When your updater functions grow complex or you have many related pieces of state that always update together,
is a natural upgrade. The reducer is itself a pure function
— structurally identical to a functional updater but centralised and easier to test.
Reducer is a pure function — trivially unit-testable
Single reducer sees all state atomically
returns the same reference React already has, so it may bail out of the re-render. Always return a new object or array.
Accidentally passing a function that returns undefined.
Using direct form when the new value depends on previous.
is correct only when called once per event and only needs the snapshot.
Putting side effects inside the updater.
Network requests, DOM manipulation, or non-idempotent logging inside
can run twice in Strict Mode. Keep updaters pure.
Because React Testing Library wraps events in
automatically, the entire update queue is flushed before the assertion runs. No extra

Custom hooks that expose a setter should nearly always use the functional form internally, because callers may invoke the hook's actions from event handlers, effects, or async callbacks — the hook cannot know the call site. Using the functional form makes the hook safe by default.
uses the direct form because it replaces with a known constant, while
use the functional form because they compute from the previous value. The
wrappers are safe with empty deps because neither callback closes over
— they read it via the updater instead.
Functional Updates and useCallback: Avoiding Stale Deps
When a callback is memoised with
, including the state variable in the dependency array causes the callback to be re-created on every state change — potentially breaking downstream
optimisations. The functional form lets you drop the state variable from the deps entirely.
The stable version is especially valuable when passing
to a memoised child component — the child won't re-render just because the counter incremented.
Functional Updates with Complex Nested Objects

When state is a nested object, the updater spreads only the layers that change — leave untouched branches as-is. Because JavaScript spread is shallow, deeply nested updates require explicit spreading at each level.
If nested spreads become verbose or error-prone, consider flattening state into independent
. Libraries like Immer let you write mutative-looking code that is transparently converted to immutable updates.

Updater functions can be defined outside the component and reused, since they are pure functions of the previous state. This approach keeps component logic thin and the core state transitions unit-testable in isolation.
This pattern — pure updater factories that return
functions — is effectively a lightweight manual reducer. When the number of transitions grows, moving to
consolidates them into one place without changing the underlying mental model.

) whenever the new value depends on the previous one.
, not the frozen snapshot — so multiple updaters chain correctly in one render cycle.
Always return a new value; never mutate the
Keep updater functions pure: no side effects, deterministic output.
When the logic grows complex or cross-variable, upgrade to
which follows the same pure-function principle at a larger scale.
